segments.text: Hello folks and welcome to today's session on the accountability mandate, which is how we navigate the software supply chain AI era.
I'm very excited to be joined today by the Director of Product at JFrog, Ronny Belenitsky. Ronnie, welcome.
Hey, nice to be here. Thank you for having me.
Of course. I'm excited about this conversation because I know that accountability when software is being developed is a hot topic.
Especially as people start using AI more and more to turbocharge their development efforts.
There's always been a need to make sure the software pipeline is secure so we're shipping good software and not causing reliability issues.
That need has really intensified as we've started to use AI as an important tool in the developer toolchain.
One thing I want to talk to you about, Ronnie, is how enterprises are addressing that tension.
The need for greater productivity that AI promises, versus the need to protect the software they're writing.
Are you seeing that coming up much in your conversation with customers?
Yeah, for sure. We're talking with a lot of enterprises and they're all mentioning the same problem.
It seems like the world has solved the bottleneck we used to have from a development perspective.
They brought in AI agents that are helping to write code.
Even some are writing all of it by themselves. However, now a new bottleneck becomes introduced.
Now we have governance bottlenecks. Enterprises are asking: "How can I be developing 10x faster..."
"...with much more code coming through, but at the end, our velocity of releases is not increasing, or even decreasing?"
That is a paradox all those organizations are asking themselves.
More than that, up until today they knew exactly who was writing the code and who the owner was.
Now AI agents are writing code, sometimes without any human catching it in the middle.
Organizations find they do not trust the end results of those AI agents.
They need to double-check what happened and have reviews by more than one person.
So the bottleneck of governance is not solved, but even amplified by AI agents.
We see that becoming more and more of an issue for all of our customers.
Teams are trying to move fast, but they're stuck. We're not delivering more; we're just writing much more code.
We're seeing similar results. We launched a developer survey in January where we asked a thousand developers about this.
One thing we learned is that there is a pretty widespread lack of complete trust in code generated by AI.
96% told us they have some level of doubt about the reliability and functional correctness of AI code.
They also told us only about 48% are always verifying the code. That’s the gap you're talking about, right?
There’s a huge volume of code, but we don't suddenly have more developers to check it.
It’s the same number of developers to check the code, and something has to give.
That’s where you get this gap where you're only able to verify some portion of it.
This is one area where there is a gap in expected versus actual velocity increases.
Do you see a difference in benefit for teams that have guardrails in place versus those that don't?
For sure. Teams without guardrails waste a lot of time doing manual reviews that stop them from moving forward.
Whereas teams with guardrails know what is expected from them and what they need to do...
...to ensure the code written by them and their agents will be trusted and eventually shipped.
This is exactly what needs to be done, and it is something that is currently missing.
Are these guardrails often internal, or are they external requirements from regulatory bodies?
They come from different levels. We have security perspectives, code quality, and compliance.
These come from the CISO, GRC managers, and R&D managers down to the teams themselves.
Teams want to "shift left" with these gates to identify problems sooner rather than later.
Tell me more about CISO guardrails, as there is often tension between separate security teams and developers.
Most security is handled by the team, but the checklist they follow comes from the CISO and AppSec.
Organizations set minimal requirements for production, like having no critical vulnerabilities.
You must have an SBOM because they want to know the origin of the code you're writing.
Compliance like SOC2 is also very important, meaning you must have provenance.
Others demand proof of code scanning and binary scanning for a full audit trail before production.
That audit trail is vital for regulatory bodies. Are there specific regulations where this is required?
Healthcare and finance are the most regulated, but we see it in other use cases now too.
In Europe, the CRA regulation starts at the end of this year with high requirements for software companies.
In the US, you have NIST. Every country has its own regulations and frameworks; it’s a huge world.
Differentiating between applications shipped to different countries is a gap that must be addressed automatically.
We talk a lot about NIST and CRA here at Sonar, especially since global customers must follow the CRA to do business in Europe.
Before we discuss the Sonar and JFrog solution, how do companies try to solve these challenges manually?
The first step is collecting evidence or attestations.
They integrate with external vendors for security, code quality, SBOMs, and end-to-end tests.
Each requires evidence at the end of the process to ensure a full audit trail.
They start by creating a "vault" of evidence for anything that goes to production.
Next is adding guardrails, specifically at the production stage, which interests the CIO and R&D heads.
Later, teams add more policies on their side through that "shift left" process.
It sounds like many disparate tools and manual processes are involved when building these from the ground up.
Yes, it’s a lot of scattered integrations. Also, you have multiple "choking points" because every team has different pipelines.
Covering all of those different pipelines is not easy for customers.
That leads to the Sonar and JFrog partnership. Many may use both but not realize what the integration offers.
Sonar leads in code scanning and quality, while JFrog leads in artifact management and binary lifecycles.
Combined, once a binary is created and stored in JFrog, we already have the Sonar reports attached as evidence.
As the binary moves toward release, we have all the Sonar data to use within the customer's guardrails.
It’s fully automatic. The customer doesn't need to build integrations; it's all in place.
Let's dive into the gates. Sonar has Quality Gates, and JFrog has Policy Gates.
The Sonar Quality Gate is the checkpoint for reliability, maintainability, and security issues.
We do software composition analysis and build SBOMs. If you pass the gate, that evidence is stored in JFrog.
Right. And on the JFrog side, you have policies around the binaries that form a version of your application.
JFrog policies ensure every part—tens or hundreds of binaries—meets the criteria, like passing Sonar gates.
We take care of the binaries from birth until they are released to production.
How is this evidence actually used? Who is using it and for what?
It’s used in policies. A CISO might say any "customer-facing" app must meet minimal requirements.
It must pass Sonar, have an SBOM, and prove provenance via Salsa.
Internal POCs might have different policies, perhaps needing no critical CVEs but ignoring code coverage.
The JFrog engine checks if the binary meets the requirement. If yes, green light. If no, it is blocked.
The developer then understands exactly what is missing to meet the criteria.
What about the GRC or compliance office? Do they report this to external regulators?
Yes. We translate CRA and NIST regulations into actual policies for R&D teams.
Requirements like provenance, SBOMs, and end-to-end tests are translated into evidence we collect.
Teams must bring these attestations at the right time in the lifecycle or they won't be able to release.
When auditors ask for proof of separation of duties or a full audit trail, it is all stored in JFrog.
You can always query it to prove you followed the rules.
Sonar also has built-in reporting for NIST, CRA, and OWASP guidelines that can be shared.
That gets packaged in the JFrog AppTrust repository so everything is in one place.
Let's talk outcomes. On the Sonar side, guardrails lead to 24% lower vulnerability rates and 12% higher quality.
We also see 18% lower rework costs. Do you have proof points from the JFrog side?
One large enterprise was spending $5-6 million annually on manual auditing and evidence gathering.
With JFrog and AppTrust, everything is continuous and automatic, saving those costs.
Another global company spent weeks manually generating a 400-page compliance PDF every year.
By shifting to automation, that manual work becomes redundant. We see a huge save for our customers.
Continuous governance removes manual "choking points" by making it part of the platform.
Manual work doesn't scale. As AI creates more code, we need these automated checkpoints.
Automation allows you to take full advantage of AI productivity without risking outages or security problems.
It’s exciting to see what customers accomplish. I look forward to the changes 2026 and 2027 will bring.
Ronnie, it’s been a pleasure speaking with you today. I really enjoyed the conversation.
Thank you. I'm very happy to be here.
To our audience, there are more sessions on the docket. Please check the agenda and ask questions.
Thank you very much and have a good day.
